# OWID V12 — Production Prompt (zero-hallucination on regions)

Prompt (~700 words): short principles + one few-shot example + metadata-aware mode (title/subtitle/note from parquet as ground truth).

Key rule: on choropleth maps, only name specific legend bands for the DARKEST clearly-separated countries; use qualitative language ('lighter shades', 'mid-range', 'not uniformly') for ambiguous regions. Reduces band-boundary hallucinations (e.g., 'North America is 0-10' when actually 0-20).

---

## `infectious-and-parasitic-diseases-death-rate-who-mdb`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

**Metadata (injected as ground truth):**

- **title**: Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate
- **subtitle**: The reported annual death rate from [infectious](#dod:communicable-diseases) and [parasitic diseases](#dod:parasitic-infection) per 100,000 people, based on the [underlying cause](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death) listed on death certificates.
- **note**: To allow for comparisons between countries and over time, this metric is [age-standardized](#dod:age_standardized). All deaths in a country may not have been registered with a [cause of death](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death).

**V12 caption (208 words):**

Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate

The reported annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people, based on the underlying cause listed on death certificates.

This line chart tracks the reported annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people from 1950 to 2023 for six specific countries. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 400. South Africa is a distinct outlier, with data beginning around 1997 near 125, rising sharply to a peak just below 400 around 2006, and declining to approximately 130 by 2023. The other five countries remain clustered in the lower portion of the chart, generally below 75. Brazil starts near 65 in 1980 and trends downward to roughly 30 by 2023. The United States begins near 80 in 1950, drops significantly to below 20 by 1970, and fluctuates between 10 and 20 for the remainder of the period. Russia, France, Germany, and Australia all show lines situated near the bottom of the chart, mostly staying within the 0 to 25 range throughout the observed years. The metric is age-standardized to allow for comparisons between countries and over time, though the data notes that not all deaths in a country may have been registered with a cause of death.

---

## `death-rates-from-mental-and-substance-disorders-by-age-who`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

**Metadata (injected as ground truth):**

- **title**: Mental health and substance use disorders death rate by age group
- **subtitle**: Estimated annual number of deaths by age group from mental health and substance use disorders per 100,000 people in the same age group. These include alcohol use, drug use, and eating disorders. Figures do not include deaths resulting from suicide, which are related to these disorders.

**V12 caption (245 words):**

Mental health and substance use disorders death rate by age group

Estimated annual number of deaths by age group from mental health and substance use disorders per 100,000 people in the same age group. These include alcohol use, drug use, and eating disorders. Figures do not include deaths resulting from suicide, which are related to these disorders.

This horizontal bar chart displays the estimated annual number of deaths per 100,000 people attributed to mental health and substance use disorders across six distinct age groups for the year 2021. The data covers alcohol use, drug use, and eating disorders, while explicitly excluding deaths resulting from suicide. The age group of 50 to 69 years records the highest death rate on the chart, with a value of 9.2 per 100,000 people. The second-highest rate is observed in the 70 years and older category, which shows a value of 6.9. The "All ages" aggregate and the 15 to 49 years group display similar mid-range values, recorded at 4.4 and 4.5 respectively. In contrast, the younger demographics show significantly lower figures. The 5 to 14 years age group has a rate of less than 0.1, represented by a very short bar. The 0 to 4 years age group records a value of 0, indicating no estimated deaths in this category for the displayed metric. The visual arrangement highlights a peak in mortality rates during middle age and older adulthood, with a sharp decline in rates for children and adolescents.

---

## `share-who-are-worried-about-vs-share-of-deaths-from-terrorism`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

**Metadata (injected as ground truth):**

- **title**: Share who are worried about vs. share of deaths from terrorism
- **subtitle**: Respondents who said they worried "very much" or "a great deal". Confirmed deaths, including all victims and attackers who died because of a [terrorist attack](#dod:terrorism).

**V12 caption (262 words):**

Share who are worried about vs. share of deaths from terrorism

Respondents who said they worried "very much" or "a great deal". Confirmed deaths, including all victims and attackers who died because of a terrorist attack.

This scatter plot compares the share of people worried about terrorism against the share of deaths from terrorism for various countries, with data points color-coded by continent. The vertical axis measures the share of people worried, ranging from 30% to over 90%, while the horizontal axis plots the share of deaths from terrorism on a logarithmic scale from 0.0001% to 1%. Circle sizes correspond to the number of attacks, with reference markers for 200 and 500 attacks. Iraq and Nigeria appear in the upper right quadrant, indicating both high shares of deaths (approaching or exceeding 1%) and high levels of worry (around 90%). Myanmar and the Maldives show high worry levels above 90% despite lower shares of deaths. In contrast, countries like New Zealand, the Netherlands, and Canada sit near the bottom of the chart with worry levels between 25% and 30% and very low shares of deaths. A cluster of European nations, including Germany, the United Kingdom, and Greece, occupies the lower-left area with worry levels between 40% and 60%. Asian countries display a wide spread; China shows moderate worry around 60% with minimal deaths, while Thailand and Kuwait show lower worry levels. The visualization highlights a general trend where higher shares of deaths often correlate with higher shares of worried respondents, though significant outliers exist where worry levels remain high despite lower mortality rates.

---

## `cumulative-number-of-objects-launched-into-outer-space`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

**Metadata (injected as ground truth):**

- **title**: Cumulative number of objects launched into space
- **subtitle**: This includes satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements launched into Earth orbit or beyond.
- **note**: When an object is launched by a country on behalf of another one, it is attributed to the latter.

**V12 caption (237 words):**

Cumulative number of objects launched into space

This includes satellites, probes, landers, crewed spacecrafts, and space station flight elements launched into Earth orbit or beyond.

This line chart tracks the cumulative number of objects launched into space from 1957 through 2025, with the vertical axis ranging from 0 to 25,000. The "World" total, represented by the highest orange line, shows a steady increase from the late 1950s, reaching approximately 5,000 by 1990 and accelerating sharply after 2010 to cross the 25,000 mark by the end of the timeline. The United States, shown in a brown line, follows a similar trajectory but at a lower volume, ending just above 15,000. Russia’s line, in blue, rises more gradually, plateauing near 4,000 by 2025. China’s purple line remains near zero until the 2000s before climbing steeply to roughly 2,000. The United Kingdom, Japan, France, India, Germany, and the European Space Agency occupy the lowest bands of the chart, with all lines remaining below 1,000 throughout the period; Japan and the European Space Agency reach approximately 500 and 300 respectively, while others cluster closer to the baseline. The note specifies that when an object is launched by a country on behalf of another one, it is attributed to the latter. The visual separation between the top three entities—World, United States, and Russia—and the remaining countries is distinct, with the latter group forming a tight cluster near the bottom of the chart.

---

## `green-climate-gcf-fund-pledges`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

**Metadata (injected as ground truth):**

- **title**: Financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund
- **subtitle**: The total financial support provided to developing countries for climate change mitigation and adaptation. Countries have set a collective goal of mobilizing $100 billion per year from 2020 onwards.
- **note**: This is measured in current US dollars per year.

**V12 caption (223 words):**

Financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund

The total financial support provided to developing countries for climate change mitigation and adaptation. Countries have set a collective goal of mobilizing $100 billion per year from 2020 onwards.

This line chart tracks the total financial support provided through the Green Climate Fund from 2011 to 2022, measured in current US dollars per year. The vertical axis ranges from $0 to $80 billion, with grid lines marking $10 billion intervals. A single blue line labeled "World" plots the annual totals. In 2011 and 2012, the line sits just below the $30 billion mark. A sharp rise occurs in 2013, bringing the value to approximately $41 billion. The line continues a gradual upward trend through 2016, reaching just above $50 billion. Between 2017 and 2020, the values fluctuate within a narrow band, dipping slightly in 2017, rising to a local peak near $56 billion in 2018, and settling around $53 billion in 2019 and 2020. From 2020 to 2022, the line shows a steep and continuous ascent, crossing the $60 billion mark in 2021 and ending at the chart's highest point in 2022, just below the $80 billion grid line. The subtitle notes a collective goal of mobilizing $100 billion per year from 2020 onwards; the plotted line remains below this threshold throughout the displayed period.

---

## `above-or-below-extreme-poverty-line-world-bank`

<img src="data:image/png;base64," width="700" />

**Metadata (injected as ground truth):**

- **title**: Number of people living in extreme poverty
- **subtitle**: Extreme poverty is defined as living below the [International Poverty Line](#dod:international-poverty-line) of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
- **note**: This data is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices. Depending on the country and year, it relates to income (measured after taxes and benefits) or to consumption, [per capita](#dod:per-capita).

**V12 caption (257 words):**

Number of people living in extreme poverty

Extreme poverty is defined as living below the International Poverty Line of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This stacked area chart illustrates the global population distribution between those living in extreme poverty and those not, spanning the years 1990 to 2026. The vertical axis measures the number of people in billions, ranging from 0 to 9 billion. The lower section, shaded in dark pink, represents the population living in extreme poverty. In 1990, this group stood at approximately 2.3 billion people. Over the subsequent decades, this area shows a consistent downward trend, dropping below 2 billion around 2005 and falling below 1 billion by 2015. By the end of the timeline in 2026, the dark pink area narrows significantly, indicating a value well below 1 billion. Conversely, the upper section, shaded in light orange, represents the population not in extreme poverty. This area demonstrates substantial growth, expanding from roughly 3 billion in 1990 to over 8 billion in 2026. The total height of the combined areas rises steadily from just over 5 billion in 1990 to approximately 8.3 billion in 2026, reflecting overall global population growth. The data presented is expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices and relates to income or consumption per capita depending on the specific country and year. The visual trajectory highlights a shift where the majority of the global population moves out of the extreme poverty category while the total number of people increases.

---
